FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly discusses the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study and its mobile/wearable technology and social media data collection plans; mentions NIH funding, 21-site structure, and youth ages 9-10 at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Paper is a perspective/commentary piece outlining current, future, and potential use of mobile and wearable technologies in the ABCD study. It does not report any original empirical analysis of ABCD data — no regression models, no statistical results, no outcome analyses. The paper describes plans and pilot feasibility data in narrative form without testing any hypothesis or reporting inferential statistics.

notes: Excluded under FT-EC3. This is a perspective paper in Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience describing technologies that are currently used or could be integrated into ABCD in the future. Some preliminary feasibility statistics (e.g., 88.5% of youth found iPads easy to use) are descriptive only and do not constitute an inferential analysis of smartphone/SM use and an individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC1.evidence: The cohort studied is the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) Study (www.abcd-study.nl), a Dutch community-based cohort of children born 2003-2004 in Amsterdam, Netherlands. The methods section states: 'This study was embedded in the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) Study... a community-based prospective cohort study that examines the relationship of maternal lifestyle and psychosocial determinants during pregnancy.' N=2354 Dutch children aged 5-6 years. This is not the U.S. NIH-funded ABCD Study.

notes: Excluded under FT-EC1. ABCD acronym here stands for Amsterdam Born Children and their Development, a Dutch cohort, not the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly uses the 5.0 data release of the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; mentions N=11,878 children at baseline (mean age 9.9 years), NIH funding with listed award numbers, 21 participating sites, and NDA Collection #2573.

FT_IC5.evidence: Paper reports an original empirical cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression analysis of ABCD data (N=9475) examining predictors of early smartphone ownership (<10 years old), with ORs and 95% CIs reported in Table 2.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatrics (2026; 157(2):e2025073891). No evidence of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The qualifying smartphone variable is age of first smartphone ownership / early smartphone ownership (<10 years old), which qualifies as a phone variable (smartphone ownership). However, the other side of every analysis consists exclusively of demographic variables: youth sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parental marital status, and parental education. Table 2 reports ORs for early smartphone ownership predicted by these demographic variables only. No individual-trait-level variable (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, personality, sleep, academic, etc.) appears on either side of any analysis in the paper. Demographic-only on the other side fails FT-IC2 per the binding rule.

notes: Excluded under FT-EC2. Paper examines sociodemographic predictors of early smartphone ownership; the smartphone variable qualifies under Step 1 (age of first smartphone), but the other side of all analyses is purely demographic (sex, race/ethnicity, income, household structure, parental education). No individual-trait-level outcome or predictor is included anywhere in the paper.
